$$
\tau = 0 . 0 0 0 6 1 .
$$

# 14.5Problems

1.Find the amplitudes of the two forced oscillations discussed in Section 14.3.

2. Find the periods of the free oscillations of (14.4.5) and (14.4.6).

3.Write down the equation for the variation of longitude, taking into account the secular acceleration of the Moon.Discuss the solutions and the orders of magnitude of any modifications to the earlier theory.

4.If the center of mass of the Moon is constrained to describea circle,with a uniform angular velocity $\pmb { \mathcal { T } } _ { \pmb { \mathcal { E } } }$ about a fixed center of force $o$ attracting according to Newton's law, show that the axis GA of the Moon will oscillate on each side of GO or will make complete revolutions relative to GO,according as the angular velocity of the Moon about its axis at the moment when GA and GO coincide in direction is less or greater than (n+w）,where w has the meaning given to it in Section 14.3. Find also the extent of the oscillations.

5.A body free to turn about its center of gravity,which is fixed,is in stable equilibrium under the attraction of a distant fixed particle $\vec { \iiint } ^ { t }$ at distance $R$ Show that the axis of least moment is turned toward the particle. Show also that the times of the principle oscillations are

$$
2 \pi \left\{ \frac { B R ^ { 3 } } { 3 M ^ { \prime } G ( C - A ) } \right\} ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } / 2 } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad 2 \pi \left\{ \frac { C R ^ { 3 } } { 3 M ^ { \prime } G ( B - A ) } \right\} ^ { \frac { 1 / 2 } { 3 / 2 } }
$$

If the body be the Earth,and $\sqrt { 5 } = \sqrt { 5 }$ the $\mathtt { S a m }$ show that the smaller of these two periods is about ten years.

# Appendix A

# Properties of Conics

# A.1 General Properties

The word “conic"comes from the phrase “conic section.” Suppose that we have a circle and a point V which is not in the plane of the circle; then lines containing V and points on the circle describe a cone. Any plane section of this cone will be a conic,and any conic can be constructed in this way. (See Figure A.1.)

The graph of the general equation of second degree in cartesian coordinates is (if there are real points satisfying the equation) a conic. Conics can be defined in this way,and their properties discussed through the properties of quadratic forms. One property is that by a transfer of origin and a rotation of the axes, we can usually reduce the equation to the form

$$
a x ^ { 2 } + b y ^ { 2 } + c = 0 .
$$

If this is possible (one exception is the parabola) then the origin of coordinates is called the center of the conic, and the coordinate axes are the axes of the conic.For work in cartesian coordinates it is usually simplest to use these axes.

Another important definition can be derived as follows. Pick one of the conic sections from Figure A.1 and insert between the section and the vertex thelargest possible sphere; this will touch the cone ina circle,C.Let the plane containing the circle be called II. The line of intersection of II and the plane of the conic section is called a directrix of the conic. Let the sphere touch the plane of the conic section at a point $\mathcal { S }$ ；this is $\mathbf { a }$ focus of the conic. See Figure A.2.

Let P be a point on the conic,and let the line VP intersect the circle C at the point A.The lines $\mathcal { P } S$ and $P A$ touch the sphere at $S$ and $A ,$ and therefore $\ P S = \ P A$ Nowdrop a perpendicular from $P$ onto the plane II,meeting the plane at Q. Let the halfangle of the cone be 0. Then from the triangle PQA we have AP = PQsec0. Let the angle between I and the plane of section be,and let R bea point on the directrix such that PR is perpendicular to the directrix. Then from the triangle $P Q R ,$ $P Q = P R \sin \phi$ Combining these results, $P S = ( \sec \theta \sin \phi ) P R$ These constructions are illustrated in Figure A.2. The result can be put into words as follows: a conic is the set of points $P$ such that the ratio of the distance of $P$ from a fixed point (a focus) to the distance of $P$ from $\hat { \mathbf { z } }$ fixed line ( $\hat { \bf \Phi }$ directrix） is constant.

![](images/4c8f8501445280c2a2d5d5d0414d305ef33f277959a50bb1eb735e7a1ef98cb3.jpg)  
Figure A.1

To find the equation of the conic in polar coordinates, choose the focus as origin,and let the polar angle, $\mathfrak { V }$ be measured from the line $0 3 3$ perpendicular to the directrix. See Figure A.3. Then if $P \{ \gamma , v \}$ is a point on the conic,

$$
r = e \{ \Bbbk - r \cos v \}
$$

where $\Bbbk$ is the distance of the origin from the directrix and $\mathcal { E }$ is the constant of proportionality: it turns out to be the eccentricity of the conic. If $\mathfrak { P } = \mathcal { e } \mathscr { k }$ ： called the parameter of the conic, then the equation can be put into the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \mathfrak { P } } { \mathfrak { r } } = 1 + \mathfrak { e } \cos \mathfrak { v } . } \end{array}
$$

![](images/6e243cca19e3605bd8c074d3e80c8b423fd7dcdf14ae36bc687fa2c7f4b3dc11.jpg)  
Figure A.2

![](images/64a3b9e9de4eb9e7eba57f78db06e61638cc8b00adfaaecca3e88c5cc9457c47.jpg)  
Figure A.3

The line OD,being an axis of symmetry，is an axis of the conic.The minimum value of $\mathscr { r }$ occurs when $v = \emptyset$ andthe maximum,if it exists,when $v = \pi$ The chord through $o$ ,perpendicular to $0 D$ ,is called the latus rectum and has length $\mathfrak { Z } \mathfrak { P }$ $\mathcal { P }$ is often referred to as the semilatus rectum. (In the constructions of Apollonius (c.2Oo B.C.) a rectangle was formed based on a line tangent to the conic and sticking out from the cone; this line has length $2 p$ It was called $\partial \rho \theta i \alpha$ meaning erect, and the original Latin translation was latus erectum. The phrase used today is a corruption of this.)

# A.2 The Ellipse

The ellipse is a conic for which $0 \leq e < 1$ If $e = \emptyset$ the ellipse becomes a circle. From the polar equation we see that $\nsucc$ is bounded,so that the ellipse is a closed figure. It follows from the theory of quadratic forms that the cartesian equation of an ellipse can be put into the form

$$
{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { b ^ { 2 } } } = 1 .
$$

$\alpha$ and $\eth$ are positive, and $\pmb { \mathcal { Q } }$ is invariably chosen to be greater than or equal to $j .$ The $z ^ { \star }$ and $y$ -axes are the major and minor axes of the ellipse, respectively, and $\pmb { \mathcal { Q } }$ and $b$ are the semimajor and semiminor axes.

From an examination of the polar equation, (A.1.2),we see that the focus used as origin lies on the $x$ -axis,and that the directrix is parallel to the $\mathcal { Y }$ -axis. Let the focus be $S$ ； then，by symmetry, there must be another focus $S ^ { \prime }$ such that the center $C$ bisects $S S ^ { \prime }$ Let the axes meet the ellipse at $A , A ^ { \prime } , B$ and $B ^ { t }$ (see Figure A.4)； then it is easily verified that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { C A = C A ^ { \prime } = a , } \\ & { C B = C B ^ { \prime } = b , } \\ & { S A = q = a ( 1 - e ) , } \\ & { S A ^ { \prime } = q ^ { \prime } = a ( 1 + e ) , } \\ & { C S = C S ^ { \prime } = a e , } \\ & { p = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { \underline { { b } } ^ { 2 } = a ^ { 2 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { S B = a . } \end{array}
$$

The polar equation can be written

$$
\frac { a \{ \bar { 1 } - e ^ { 2 } \} } { \uparrow } = \bar { 1 } \div e \cos v .
$$

These formulas should all be memorized.

Another definition of the ellipse is that it is the locus of $\mathcal { P }$ such that

$$
S _ { \vartheta } ^ { \mathcal { P } } + S ^ { \prime } \mathcal { P } = { \cos } { \tan } { \tan } { \tan } { \iota _ { 3 } }
$$

where $S$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ are fixed points; clearly these are the foci, and the constant has the value $2 a$ To see this, refer to Figure A.5 where two spheres have been inserted into the cone, one above and one below the elipse; these touch the ellipse at points $s$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ ,and the cone in circles $C$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ Let $\mathcal P$ be any point on the ellipse,and let the line VP through the vertex of the cone meet $C$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ in points $A$ and $A ^ { \prime }$ ,respectively. $\pmb { \mathcal { P S } }$ and PA are lines tangent to the upper sphere at S and A, so PS = PA. Similarly， PS' = PA'. So $\mathcal { P S } + \mathcal { P S } ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { P A } + \mathcal { P A } ^ { \prime }$ ,which is independent of the position of $\mathcal { P }$

![](images/5696ec9652db5e3c73a2bfdc4d6a9653af372c8db67a00e031f1b033d3c7e444.jpg)  
Figure A.4

![](images/e22778bb49b3efb32cc4b2a0cb74e0e3d0f4384dc7419c68b8a90517f27f1e5b.jpg)  
Figure A.5

The lines $S P$ and $S ^ { \prime } P$ make equal angles with the tangent at $\mathcal { P }$ See Figure A.5.Let $s$ be arc length measured along the curve,and let $S \mathcal { P } = \mathfrak { r }$ ， $S \mathcal { P } ^ { i } = \mathfrak { r } ^ { i }$ ； then from the property just noted,

$$
\frac { d \tilde { \varepsilon } } { d s } + \frac { d \tilde { c } ^ { s } } { d s } = 0 .
$$

But $d > / d \sin$ is equal to the cosine of the angle between the line $S \mathcal { P }$ and the tangent, with a similar result for $d ^ { 3 } x ^ { 8 } / d s$ The result follows at once.

From the form of the cartesian equation of the ellipse, (A.2.1),it follows that the ellipse is the orthogonal projection ofa circle of radius $\pmb { G }$ ;this is the auriliary circle.Alternatively,draw a circle of radius $\pmb { \mathcal { a } }$ and choose any diameter $A A ^ { \prime }$ From any point $a$ on the circumference,drop a perpendicular $Q R$ onto $A A ^ { 3 }$ and construct $P$ om $Q R$ such that

$$
\frac { \mathcal { P } R } { Q R } = \frac { \mathfrak { z } } { a } .
$$

![](images/a6e221c6c47d49c2d58425cf37b71255642b9d455e6e2c90aed3f004414743be.jpg)  
Figure A.6

Then $P$ will trace out the ellipse with semiaxes $a$ and $b$ (See Figure A.6.） It is easily verified from this property that the area of the ellipse is

et $\angle Q C A = E$ then the cartesian coordinates of $P$ are

$$
x = a \cos { \mathcal { E } } , \quad y = b \sin { \mathcal { E } } .
$$

$E$ is called the eccentric angle of $P$

# A.3 The Parabola

The conic with $e = \texttt { \AA }$ is the parabola. If we let $e$ tend to one for an ellipse, then $\pmb { \mathcal { a } }$ tends to infinity. Although some properties of the parabola can be deduced from corresponding properties of the ellipse by leting $e = \tt { \tt \tt { j } }$ and $1 / a =$ $0 .$ this may lead to expressions that are meaningless; as, for instance, if it is applied to (A.2.2). However, if we remember that the pericenter distance, $q = S A$ ,remains finite,and write (A.2.2) as

![](images/fd0eba9376d9ea724e815dab4c7bb718be4ab29b5303480e088e270c275d998a.jpg)  
Figure A.7

$$
\frac { g ( \mathtt { j } \div e ) } { r } = \mathtt { j } + e \cos v ,
$$

then there is no difficulty in putting $e = \mathtt { \mathtt { j } }$ , and we find the polar equation of the parabola in the usual form:

$$
\displaystyle \mathfrak { r } = \mathfrak { q } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { \mathfrak { v } } { 2 } .
$$

The size of a parabola is usually given by $q$ (All parabolas have the same shape.) The semilatus rectum is $2 q$

The cartesian equation can be put into the form

$$
y ^ { 2 } = 4 q x .
$$

There is only one focus, at $\{ q , 0 \}$ ,and one axis, the $\mathfrak { L }$ -axis.

Return for a moment to the ellipse,and let $e$ tend to one. The second focus tends to infinity,and the theorem that the tangent at $\mathcal { P }$ makes equal angles with $S P$ and $S ^ { \beta }$ becomes, for the parabola, that the tangent at $\mathbfcal { P }$ makes equal angles with $S P$ and the axis of the parabola. This leads to the wellknown property that a ray of light traveling parallel to the axis of a parabolic mirror passes through the focus after reflection. (See Figure A.7.)

Sometimes it is convenient to consider a curve as traced out by the variation ofa single variable,or parameter;the equations (A.2.3) are an example of this; they are one form (out of many possibilities) of the parametric equations of the ellipse. There are many possible representations of the parabola; perhaps the simplest is

$$
x = { q _ { \mathrm { { f } } } ^ { 2 } , y = 2 q _ { \mathrm { { } } } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

# A.4 The Hyperbola

The hyperbola is defined by $e > \frac { \ast } { \ast }$ From the polar equation we see that the curve is not bounded, since $\mathfrak { r }$ can become arbitrarily large. When $\mathfrak { F }$ does become large, $\mathfrak { V }$ tends to one of the values given by

$$
v _ { \alpha } = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { e } \right\} .
$$

The cartesian equation can be put into the form

$$
{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { \bar { b } ^ { 2 } } } = \mathtt { i } .
$$

In this section,for ease of geometrical description, $\mathfrak { a }$ and $\pmb { \dot { \theta } }$ will be taken to be positive. But note that in computation,and in formulas for Keplerian motion, ashould bea negative number.When $\pmb { \mathcal { X } }$ and $y$ become large,(A.4.2) is nearly the same as

$$
{ \bigl ( } { \frac { x } { a } } + { \frac { y } { b } } { \bigr ) } { \bigl ( } { \frac { x } { a } } - { \frac { y } { b } } { \bigr ) } = 0 ,
$$

which is the equation of -two lines through the origin. The larger $\Im ^ { * }$ becomes, the more nearly the curve resembles these two lines; they are called asymptotes.

The directions given by the values of ${ \mathcal { V } } _ { \mathcal { Q } }$ ,from (A.4.1), must be parallel to these asymptotes,and we deduce that the angle between the asymptotes is

$$
2 \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( - \frac { 1 } { e } \right) \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 } { e ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \tt i } \right) .
$$

From (A.4.2） we see that there is no point on the hyperbola for which $- a < z < a$ therefore the hyperbola has two branches;the polar equation gives only one of these. (See Figure A.8.） Comparing the gradients of the asymptotes,(A.4.3),with the formula va,we find

$$
\tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \ b { \ b } } { \ b { \ a } } } = \cos ^ { - 1 } { \frac { 1 } { e } }
$$

![](images/ed2e25d89cb2c125843b00a7292978f43e1d32f09d3f3fd9f66c18bda6ccf469.jpg)  
Figure A.8

from which we deduce

$$
\mathfrak { z } ^ { 2 } = \mathfrak { a } ^ { 2 } \{ e ^ { 2 } - 1 \} .
$$

$b$ can be less or greater than $\pmb { \mathcal { Z } }$ Figures $A . 9 ( a )$ ，(b）,and $f ( c )$ show hyperbolas for which $\dot { \bar { \boldsymbol { \theta } } }$ is greater than,equal to,and less than $a$ If $\dot { \boldsymbol { b } } = \boldsymbol { a }$ then the figure isarectangular hyperbola.

In the notation of Figure A.10, we see that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C A = a , } } \\ { { A S = g = a ( e - 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The tangent at $A$ cuts the asymptotes in points $( - a , \pm b )$ The semilatus rectum is

$$
\mathfrak { P } = \mathfrak { a } \{ \mathfrak { e } ^ { 2 } - \mathfrak { I } \} .
$$

![](images/9dd31788c670416964b77e2ae80eb262d9094641f4a4ebe6cdcc49997841d25f.jpg)  
Figure A.9

This section is required for the interpretation of Hamilton's theorem,Section 4.9.

![](images/7dbf9c50ecee1315259d5820dfdf6171f125cafb96278c085a73bd2067ae6003.jpg)  
Figure A.10

The hyperbola can be defined as the locus of P such that the difference between SP and S'P is constant, S and S' being fixed points. These are the foci, and to recover the equations given above, the constant difference must be equal to $2 a$ 、so that

$$
\displaystyle S S ^ { i } = 2 c = 2 a e .
$$

The cartesian coordinates of any point on the hyperbola with equation （A.4.2)are given by the parametric equations

$$
x = \pm a \cos ^ { \ddag } F , y = b \sin ^ { \ddag } x .
$$

# A.5Pole and Polar

Let P(x1, y) be any point from which real tangents can be drawn to a conic with the general equation

$$
a x ^ { 2 } + 2 h x y + b y ^ { 2 } + 2 g x + 2 f y + c = 0 .
$$

Let these tangents meet the conic at points Q and R; then it is not diffcult to establish that the equation of the line QR is

$$
x ( a x _ { 1 } + h y _ { 1 } + g ) + y ( b y _ { 1 } + h x _ { 1 } + f ) + g x _ { 1 } + f y _ { 1 } + c = 0 .
$$

This line is the polar of P with respect to the conic. Even if real tangents cannot be drawn from P,(A.5.2) still defines the polar of P. Conversely，P is the pole of the line with equation (A.5.2).

The reader will be able to verify without difficulty that the focus and directrix are pole and polar.

f $\mathcal { P }$ lies on the conic, then (A.5.2) is the equation of the tangent at $P$ ·

# Appendix B

# The Rotation of Axes

If a system of right-handed rectangular axes is rotated positively through $\theta$ about the $\mathscr { L }$ -axis,the new coordinates, $\left( x ^ { 2 } , y ^ { 2 } , z ^ { 3 } \right)$ ,of $\hat { \mathbf { a } }$ point are related to the old, $\{ \mathfrak { x } , \mathfrak { z } \} , z \}$ ,by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x ^ { \prime } = x , } \\ { y ^ { \prime } = y \cos \theta \div z \sin \theta , } \\ { z ^ { \prime } = - y \sin \theta + z \cos \theta . } \end{array}
$$

These equations can be written in matrix notation as

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \tau ^ { \prime } } \\ { z ^ { \prime } } \\ { z ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right] } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] }
$$

$$
{ \bf \Omega } = P ( \theta ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

where

$$
P ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { { 1 } } & { { 0 } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { \cos \theta } } & { { \sin \theta } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { - \sin \theta } } & { { \cos \theta } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Similarly,a positive rotation through $\boldsymbol { \theta }$ about the $y$ -axis leads to new coordinates given by

where

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x ^ { \prime } } \\ { y ^ { \prime } } \\ { z ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right] } = Q ( \theta ) \left[ { \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} } \right] ,
$$

$$
Q ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { 0 } & { - \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } & { 0 } \\ { \sin \theta } & { 0 } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] .
$$

And a positive rotation through $\theta$ about the $z \mathrm { . }$ axis leads to new coordinates given bv

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { { \hat { x } ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \hat { y } ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { z ^ { \prime } } } \end{array} \right] = R ( \theta ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

where

$$
R ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .
$$

For a combination of two rotations, let us say $R \{ \theta \}$ followed by $P \{ \phi \}$ ,(the order is crucially important),we have

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { z ^ { I } } \\ { y ^ { I } } \\ { z ^ { I } } \end{array} \right] = P ( \phi ) R ( \theta ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right]
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathbf { \Phi } } & { = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \phi } & { \sin \phi } \\ { 0 } & { \sin \phi \cos \phi } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] } \\ & { = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } & { 0 } \\ { - \cos \phi \sin \theta } & { \cos \phi \cos \theta } & { \sin \phi } \\ { \sin \phi \sin \theta } & { - \sin \phi \cos \theta } & { \cos \phi } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

In the same way the matrix describing any sequence of rotations can be found by straightforward matrix multiplication. For example, suppose that $\{ X , Y , Z \}$ are coordinates of a comet in the orbital reference system,with $O X$ pointing toward perihelion,and $\beta Z$ parallel to $\Bbbk$ (so that $Z = \mathfrak { g }$ ,and that $\{ x , y , z \}$ are the coordinates of the comet in an equatorial system of axes with $\mathcal { O } x$ pointing toward the vernal equinox and $\theta z$ toward the north celestial pole. Then

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] = P ( - \epsilon ) R ( - \Omega ) P ( - i ) R ( - \omega ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { X } \\ { Y } \\ { Z } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Be very careful with signs $\mathfrak { O } ^ { \sharp }$ rotations. The transformations apply equally to velocity components.They can be used to advantage with “infinitesimal" rotations. In fact their use, and the use of vector operations, almost completely obviates the need for spherical trigonometry.

# Appendix C

# Numerical Values

Numerical values continue to be refined,and further objects will be found that might be included in tables such as these.Don't be surprised if quite different values appear in other references (and even within the same reference). In some instances there are “official” values, so that different computations share a common set,making comparison possible. The following values are included partly for their interest,and partly so that you can include them,if you wish,in your computations.

# C.1Orbital Elements of Planets

The data for the planets (excluding Pluto) were kindly supplied by L. E. Doggett from data of P. Bretagnon (Reference 72). They are mean elements, referred to the equinox J2000.0. $\mathcal { T }$ ismeasured in Julian centuries from that epoch.The semimajor axes have been derived from the terms in $I$ (the mean longitude at epoch) factored by $\pmb { \mathcal { T } }$ with allowance first made for the precession in longitude. (See Appendix C7.)

For detailed information on osculating elements, refer to the Astronomical Almanac. For low-precision formulas,see Reference 38.

# Appendix D

# MISCELLANEOUS EXPANSIONS IN SERIES

# D.1 $\hat { f }$ and $\mathfrak { g }$ Series

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { f = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma t ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma \tau t ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 4 } \sigma ( 3 \omega - 2 \sigma - 1 5 \tau ^ { 2 } ) t ^ { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 8 } \sigma \tau ( 3 \omega - 2 \sigma - 7 \tau ^ { 2 } ) t ^ { 5 } } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } + \frac { 1 } { 7 2 0 } \sigma \{ ( 6 3 0 \omega - 4 2 0 \sigma - 9 4 5 \tau ^ { 2 } ) \tau ^ { 2 } - ( 2 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } - 6 6 \sigma \omega + 4 5 \omega ^ { 2 } ) \} t ^ { 6 } + \cdots } \\ & { g = t - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \sigma t ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \sigma \tau t ^ { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 2 0 } \sigma ( 9 \omega - 8 \sigma - 4 5 \tau ^ { 2 } ) t ^ { 5 } } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } - \frac { 1 } { 2 4 } \sigma \tau ( 6 \omega - 5 \sigma - 1 4 \tau ^ { 2 } ) t ^ { 6 } + \cdots } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\sigma = \frac { \vec { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } } { \tau _ { \bar { \mathfrak { d } } } ^ { 3 } } , \ \tau = \frac { \dot { \bar { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } } } { \tau _ { 0 } } , \ \omega = \frac { \dot { \bar { \mathfrak { x } } } _ { 0 } - \dot { \bar { \mathfrak { x } } } _ { 0 } } { \tau _ { \bar { \mathfrak { d } } } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

# D.2Elliptic Motion

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { E = M + 2 \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { n } { \mathrm { J } _ { n } ( n \epsilon ) } \sin n \mathrm { \Delta } d } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \cos B = - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { + } + 2 \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { n } { J _ { n } ^ { \prime } ( n \epsilon ) } \cos n \mathrm { \Delta } d } } \\ { { \sin E = \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { \epsilon } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { n } { J _ { n } ^ { \prime } ( n \epsilon ) } \sin n \mathrm { \Delta } n d } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \sin v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } m _ { ( n \epsilon ) } \sin n \mathrm { \Delta } M } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \cos v = - \epsilon + \displaystyle \frac { 2 ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) } { \epsilon } \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \mathcal { J } _ { n } ( n \epsilon ) \cos n M } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { v - M = ( 2 e - \frac { 1 } { 4 } e ^ { 3 } ) \sin M + ( \frac { 5 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 1 } { 2 4 } e ^ { 4 } ) \sin 2 M + } \\ { \frac { 1 3 } { 1 2 } e ^ { 3 } \sin 3 M + \frac { 1 0 3 } { 9 6 } e ^ { 4 } \sin 4 M + \cdots } \end{array}
$$

The $J _ { \sqrt { \pi } }$ and $J _ { \pi } ^ { \prime }$ are Bessel functions. The first few are given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { J _ { 1 } ( e ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 9 2 } e ^ { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 9 2 1 6 } e ^ { 6 } + \cdots ) , } \\ & { J _ { 2 } ( 2 e ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 3 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 4 } e ^ { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 6 0 } e ^ { 6 } + \cdots ) , } \\ & { J _ { 3 } ( 3 e ) = \frac { 9 } { 1 6 } e ^ { 3 } ( 1 - \frac { 9 } { 1 6 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 8 1 } { 6 4 0 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots ) , } \\ & { J _ { 4 } ( 4 e ) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } e ^ { 4 } ( 1 - \frac { 4 } { 5 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 } { 1 5 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots ) , } \\ & { J _ { 5 } ( 5 e ) = \frac { 6 2 5 } { 7 6 8 } e ^ { 5 } ( 1 - \frac { 2 5 } { 2 4 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 6 2 5 } { 1 3 4 4 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots ) , } \\ & { J _ { 6 } ( 6 e ) = \frac { 8 1 } { 8 0 } e ^ { 6 } ( 1 - \frac { 9 } { 7 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 8 1 } { 1 1 2 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots ) , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { { J } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } ( e ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } { { \left( 1 - \frac { 3 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 5 } { 1 9 2 } e ^ { 4 } - \frac { 7 } { 9 2 1 6 } e ^ { 6 } + \cdots \right) } } , } \\ & { { { J } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } ( 2 e ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e { \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { 3 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 9 0 } e ^ { 6 } + \cdots \right) } , } \\ & { { { J } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } } ( 3 e ) = \frac { 9 } { 1 6 } e ^ { 3 } { { \left( 1 - \frac { 1 5 } { 1 6 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 8 9 } { 6 4 0 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots \right) } } , } \\ & { { { J } _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } } ( 4 e ) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } e ^ { 3 } { { \left( 1 - \frac { 6 } { 5 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 8 } { 1 5 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots \right) } } , } \\ & { { { J } _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } } ( 5 e ) = \frac { 6 2 5 } { 7 6 8 } e ^ { 4 } { { \left( 1 - \frac { 3 5 } { 2 4 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 7 5 } { 4 4 8 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots \right) } } , } \\ & { { { J } _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } } ( 6 e ) = \frac { 8 1 } { 8 0 } e ^ { 5 } { { \left( 1 - \frac { 1 2 } { 7 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 3 5 } { 1 1 2 } e ^ { 4 } - \cdots \right) } } . } \end{array}
$$

In general,

$$
\mathcal { I } _ { n } ( n e ) = \frac { e } { 2 } \left( \frac { n e } { 2 } \right) ^ { n - 1 } \frac { 1 } { ( n - 1 ) ! } \left\{ 1 - \frac { n ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } { 2 \cdot ( 2 n + 2 ) } + \frac { n ^ { 4 } e ^ { 4 } } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot ( 2 n + 2 ) ( 2 n + 4 ) } - \cdots \right\} ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \mathcal J } _ { n } ^ { t } ( n e ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { n e } { 2 } \right) ^ { n - 1 } \frac { 1 } { ( n - 1 ) ! } \left\{ 1 - \frac { n + 2 } { n } \frac { n ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 2 n + 2 ) } \right. } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle \left. ~ + \frac { n + 4 } { n } \frac { n ^ { 4 } e ^ { 4 } } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot ( 2 n + 2 ) ( 2 n + 4 ) } - \cdots \right\} } . } \end{array}
$$

The power series for the Bessel functions converge for all values of e. But if the series for quantities in elliptic motion are expressed explicitly as power series in $e _ { \mathrm { { s } } }$ then they converge for lel <0.6627434...See Ref.26.

# Appendix E

# The Solution of Linear Systems

Two programs are listed here. One is for solving the system Ax = b, assuming n equations for n unknowns, and the other is for inverting the matrix A. These are required particularly in connection with the improvement of orbits. The programs use Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting.Here the elimination takes place starting with the leftmost column and moving systematically to the right,but the row that contains the pivot is chosen by the program. First,the largest number (in absolute value) in each row of A is found,and this provides a weight for the row. Each possible candidate for a pivot is divided by the weight for its row; the pivot chosen is that with the largest resulting number. The program for inversion is wasteful of storage space,but is,I hope, clear.The final product of the pivots gives the value of the determinant of A. If at any stage of the program the product of the pivots used so far becomes less(in absolute value) than a small number,which the operator provides, then the system is declared to be il-conditioned, and execution stops.

40PRINT "ARE CHANGED."   
50 PRINT THE SYSTEN IS AX =B,VITH X THE VECTOR OF   
60 PRINT UNKNOWNS.THE COMPONENTS OF A AND B SHOULD BE   
70 PRINT ENTERED BY COLUMN INTO A DATA STATEMENT. IN THE   
80 PRINT "PROGRAH,B(K) IS LOCATED IN A(K,N+1).   
90 PRINT " THE PROGRAN USES GAUSSIAN ELIHINATION WITH PARTIAL"   
100PRINT PIVOTING.IT ALSO CALCULATES THE ABSOLUTE VALUE OF   
110 PRINT "THE DETERNINANT. IF AT ANY STAGE THE ABSOLUTE VALUE   
120 PRINT "OF THE PRODUCT OF THE PIVOTS IS SMALLER THAN A NUNBER"   
130 PRINT "EPS，TO BE ENTERED BY THE OPERATOR,THE PROGRAK WILL   
140PRINT "END. :PRINT   
150 PRINT PRESS A KEY TO CONTINUE."   
160 A\$ $\Bumpeq$ INKEYS:IF AS $\mathbf { \Psi } =$ THEN 160

# Appendix F

# The Generation on the Computer of Gaussian Deviates

Functions that generate sample values of a rectangular probability distribution (usually, one where all numbers between O and 1 are equally probable) are routinely available in computer software. In order to model errors of observation, it is necessary to generate sample values of a Gaussian (or normal) distribution; these sample values are called Gaussan deviates. Gauss was able to deduce the distribution theoretically for errors of observation under the hypothesis that an error is formed by the simple addition of a large number of small independent elementary errors.In the program that follows, just twelve sample values of a rectangular distribution are added. The output,RV, is a sample value of the Gaussian distribution with r.m.s. value S,and mean value (usually assumed to be zero for observational errors) M. The syntax is IBM BASIC.

10RANDOMIZE   
20 $\Re \Im \mathrm { ~ \pm ~ } \Im$   
30 FOR $z \ = \ \frac { 4 } { 3 }$ T0 12   
40 $R _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } V = R _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } \times \mathbf { \mathbf { \vec { D } } } \neq R _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$   
50 NEXTI   
$6 0 ~ \mathrm { \ B V ~ = ~ ( 8 V ~ - ~ 6 ) * S ~ + ~ 1 4 }$

Of those listed, the periodic comets Reinmuth 2,1980n,and Gunn 1982 are particularly notable as having orbits that are,at times,strongly perturbed by Jupiter.You might find it of interest to investigate their orbits by numerical integration over several hundred years (forward and back in time). \*\*Minor planet 3415 was discovered in 1928 by K. Reinmuth. It was named after the author in 1986. The name also is for his daughter, Dinah, who was an assiduous worker at the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory while she was an undergraduate at Harvard.

# The Greek Alphabet
