# Appendix I

# Random Variables, and Least Squares

Let ( represent a random variable or variate. This might be the error of a particular observation (range-rate, for instance) made by a particular instrument.

Let $\textit { \textbf { z } }$ represent a sample value of $\zeta$

The sample space of ( is the set of all possible sample values;here, we shall take it to be $- \infty < z < \infty$

Theprobability distribution function of $\zeta$ is

$$
F _ { \zeta } ( z ) = P r [ \{ z ^ { \prime } ; z ^ { \prime } \leq z \} ]
$$

i.e.,“the probability that a sample point z' has a value less than or equal to a specifed number z.” In particular,F(-oo)=0,and F(oo)=1.(A probablility of1 stands for certainty.）

Assuming F(z) to be differentiable, the probablility density function ofis

$$
\dot { \phi } _ { 5 } ( z ) = \frac { d \bar { F } _ { \zeta } ( z ) } { d z } .
$$

From the fundamental theorem of calculus we have

$$
{ \cal F } _ { \zeta } ( z ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { z } \phi _ { \zeta } ( z ^ { \prime } ) d z ^ { \prime } .
$$

Then also

$$
\phi _ { \zeta } ( z ) d z = P r [ \{ z ^ { \prime } ; z < z ^ { \prime } < z + d z \} ] ,
$$

i.e.,the probablity that a sample point z' lies in the interval z < z' < z+dz. Also

$$
\int _ { \bar { \diamond } } ^ { a } \phi _ { \zeta } \{ z ^ { \prime } \} d z ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { P r } [ \{ z ^ { \prime } ; b < z ^ { \prime } < a ) \} .
$$

In particular,

$$
\int \limits _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \phi _ { 5 } ( z ^ { \prime } ) d z ^ { \prime } = I .
$$

The expected value (or first moment or statistical average) of $\zeta$ is

$$
E [ \zeta ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } z \phi _ { \zeta } ( z ) d z .
$$

This is the bias of $5$ often referred to as systematic error. The variate is said to be unbiased if $E \{ \phi \} = \emptyset$

Knowledge of the“spread” of the probability density is given by the second moment or variance:

$$
\sigma ^ { 2 } = E [ \{ \zeta - E _ { \downarrow } ^ { \uparrow } \zeta ] \} ^ { 2 } ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } ( z - E [ \zeta ] ) ^ { 2 } \phi _ { \zeta } ( z ) d z .
$$

$\sigma$ is called the root mean square deviation.

The phrase “the expected value of...,” or its corresponding symbolic representation, $E _ { \downarrow } ^ { \prime } , \ldots ]$ can be considered as an operator. If $f ( \zeta )$ is $\mathfrak { a }$ non-random function,then its expected value is defined to be

$$
E [ f ( \zeta ) ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f ( z ) \dot { \phi } _ { \zeta } ( z ) d z .
$$

A function of $\mathbf { a }$ random variable is itself a random variable. In particular, since observations are random variables,so are the parameters that are estimated through the use of the observations.

The normal distribution is defined by

$$
\phi _ { \zeta } ( z ) = \frac { \mathbb { i } } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \hbar } \exp \left( - \frac { \mathbb { i } } { 2 } \frac { ( z - z _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } } { \hbar ^ { 2 } } \right) .
$$

Then $E _ { \mathrm { { i } } } ^ { \mathrm { { f } } } \zeta \} = z _ { 0 }$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 }$ .Of the two parameters $h$ and $z _ { \Updownarrow }$ ， $\hslash$ is taken to be strictly positive. The factor preceding the exponential is calculated so that (I.1) should be true.For large $\hslash$ the distribution has a relatively low maximum,and a wide spread;for small $\hbar$ thedistribution falls off sharply form themaximum.

The normal distribution is often called the Gaussian distribution, and the corresponding variate $\zeta$ isa Gaussian variate.It dates back to its invention by de Moivre, in 1733,but acquired its present importance in the hands of Gauss in 1809.Gauss deduced the distribution theoretically for errors under the hypothesis that an error is formed by the simple addition of small independent elementary errors; these elementary errors are of the same order of magnitude and are as likely to be positive as negative.

Next, consider two random variables $\mathfrak { P }$ and $\zeta$ with sample values $\mathscr { Y }$ and $z$ respectively. The joint probability function is

$$
F _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) = P r [ \{ y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } ; y ^ { \prime } \leq y , z ^ { \prime } \leq z \} ] .
$$

i.e., the probablility that sample values $y ^ { i }$ and $z ^ { \prime }$ satisfy both $y ^ { \prime } \leq y$ and $z ^ { \prime } \leq z$ for specified $y$ and $z$ The joint density function is

$$
\phi _ { \mathfrak { V } } \zeta ( y , z ) = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F _ { \mathfrak { V } } \zeta ( y , z ) } { \partial y \partial z } ,
$$

assuming that allnecessary derivatives exist.

The first moments are

and

$$
 \begin{array} { r } { \mu _ { \eta } = E [ \eta ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } y \phi _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) d y d z ] } \\ { \quad } \\ { \mu _ { \zeta } = E [ \zeta ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } z \phi _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) d y d z . } \end{array} |
$$

Second moments are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \sigma _ { \eta } ^ { 2 } = E [ ( \eta - E [ \eta ] ) ^ { 2 } ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } ( y - E [ \eta ] ) ^ { 2 } \phi _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) d y d z , } \\ & { \sigma _ { \zeta } ^ { 2 } = E [ ( \zeta - E [ \zeta ] ) ^ { 2 } ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } ( z - E [ \zeta ] ) ^ { 2 } \phi _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) d y d z , } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ a n d } } \\ & { \mu _ { \eta \zeta } = E \{ ( \eta - E [ \eta ] ) ( \zeta - E [ \zeta ] ) \} = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } ( y - E [ \eta ] ) ( z - E [ \zeta ] ) \phi _ { \eta \zeta } ( y , z ) d y d z . } \end{array}
$$

If the variates are independent, so that we can write

$$
\phi _ { \eta _ { 3 } \zeta } ( y , z ) = \phi _ { \eta _ { 3 } } ( y ) \phi _ { \zeta } ( z ) ,
$$

then the variates are called statistically independent. In this case, pnc =0.

Correlation between n and ( is specified by the number

$$
{ \rho = \frac { { \mu } _ { 9 5 } } { \sigma _ { 9 } \ \sigma _ { \zeta } } }
$$

called the linear correlation coeffeient. If the variates are statistically independent.o= O: if thev are linearly related,then p=±1.

The normal distribution can be generalized for a bivariate distribution in terms of its first and second moments as follows.

Let

$$
{ \cal Q } = \left[ { \sigma _ { \it 3 5 } ^ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } { \mu _ { \it 3 5 } } \right] .
$$

This is called the covariance matrix of the distribution. Then we can write

$$
\phi _ { \eta , \zeta } ( y , z ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi | Q | ^ { 1 / 2 } } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ y - \mu _ { \eta } ~ z - \mu _ { \zeta } ] Q ^ { - 1 } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { { y - \mu _ { \eta } } } \\ { { z - \mu _ { \zeta } } } \end{array} \right] \right) .
$$

$\alpha$ is the determinant of $Q$

This form is convenient for the generalization to any number of joint variates. Let S denote a column matrix, or vector, having components SiS2,..,Sn. The ：.

probablility density function is a scalar function ${ \phi } _ { \zeta } ( \mathbf { z } )$ ， $\pmb { \mathrm { z } }$ now being a vector. The expected value of $\pmb { \mathcal { Z } }$ is a vector having as its $\dot { z }$ -th component

$$
E [ \zeta _ { i } ] = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } z _ { i } \phi _ { \zeta } ( \mathbf { z } ) d z _ { 1 } d z _ { 2 } \ldots d z _ { \Re } ,
$$

so the whole vector can be conveniently written as

$$
E \{ \zeta \} = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \Xi \phi _ { \zeta } ( { \bf z } ) d z .
$$

A typical second order moment is

$$
\mu _ { i j } = E _ { i } ^ { \uparrow } ( \zeta _ { i } - E _ { i } ^ { \uparrow } \zeta _ { i } ] ) ( \zeta _ { j } - E [ \zeta _ { j } ] ) ^ { \uparrow } ,
$$

so that all the second moments are contained in the symmetric matrix

$$
Q = E [ \zeta - E [ ( \zeta ] ) ( \zeta - E [ \zeta ] ) ^ { T } ] ,
$$

which is the covariance matriz of the distribution. It has elements $\mu _ { i j }$ ,where $\mu _ { i j } = \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 }$

The correlation matrix has typical element

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho _ { i j } = \frac { \mathcal { \mu } _ { i j } } { \sigma _ { i } \sigma _ { j } } . } \end{array}
$$

The normal distribution in this case is

$$
\phi _ { \zeta } ( \mathbf { z } ) = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi ) ^ { \pi / 2 } | Q | ^ { 1 / 2 } } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \mathbf { z } - E [ \zeta ] ) ^ { T } Q ^ { - 1 } ( \mathbf { z } - E [ \zeta ] ) \right) .
$$

Now consider the problem of estimating the $\curvearrowleft$ components of $\mathbf { x }$ ,the parameters specifying an orbit, from the $\mathfrak { P }$ components of $\mathbb { Y }$ ，the observations. Given $\mathbf { x }$ ,observations free of error can be found from

$$
\mathbb { Y } _ { c } = \mathbb { Y } ( \mathbb { X } ) .
$$

Let $s$ be the variate denoting the observational error, having a normal distribution without bias:

$$
\acute { \varphi } \zeta ^ { ( \mathbf { z } ) } = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi ) ^ { \pi / 2 } ] Q ^ { | \mathbf { \bar { z } } / \mathbf { \bar { z } } } } \exp \left( - \frac { \mathrm { i } } { 2 } \mathbf { z } ^ { T } Q ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { z } \right) .
$$

Making an estimate of $\mathbf { x }$ is equivalent to estimating the error of $\mathbb { Y }$ The likelihood of $^ \mathrm { a }$ given estimate $\mathbb { x }$ is

$$
\vec { \mathcal { L } } ( \mathbf { X } ) = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi ) ^ { n / 2 } | Q | ^ { 1 / 2 } } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ ( \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { e } ( \mathbf { X } ) ] ^ { T } Q ^ { - 1 } [ \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } ) ] \right) .
$$

where Y。 contains the actual observations. The “best” estimate of X is that which maximizes the likelihood. In the present case, this is equavelent to minimizing the variance, or the argument of the exponential. Let the vector operator $\nabla _ { \mathbf x }$ be defined by

$$
\nabla _ { \mathbf { X } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \partial / \partial X _ { 1 } } \\ { \partial / \partial X _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { \partial / \partial X _ { m } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Then for $\mathbb { Z } ( \mathbb { X } )$ to have a maximum at ${ \mathbb X } = { \mathbb X } ^ { * }$ ,it is necessary that

$$
\biggl ( \nabla _ { \mathbf { X } } \left[ \mathbf { Y } ( \mathbf { X } ) \right] ^ { T } Q ^ { - 1 } \Bigl [ \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } ) \Bigr ] \biggr ) _ { \mathbf { X } = \mathbf { X } ^ { * } } = 0 .
$$

This equation is solved through lineariation, as in section (7.5). We let

$$
\mathbf { y } = \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } ) .
$$

Also

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \nabla _ { \mathbf x } [ \mathbf Y _ { c } ( \mathbf X ) ] ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \partial / \partial X _ { 1 } } \\ { \partial / \partial X _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { \partial / \partial X _ { m } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { Y _ { 1 } ( \mathbf X ) Y _ { 2 } ( \mathbf X ) \dots Y _ { n } ( \mathbf X ) } \\ { \vdots } \\ { \partial / \partial X _ { m } } \end{array} \right] } \\ { = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \partial Y _ { 1 } / \partial X _ { 1 } \partial _ { 2 } / \partial X _ { 1 } \dots \partial Y _ { n } / \partial X _ { 1 } } \\ { \partial Y _ { 1 } / \partial X _ { 2 } \partial _ { 2 } / \partial X _ { 2 } \dots \partial Y _ { n } / \partial X _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } & { \vdots \vdots } \\ { \partial Y _ { 1 } / \partial X _ { m } \partial _ { 2 } / \partial X _ { m } \dots \partial Y _ { n } / \partial X _ { m } } \end{array} \right] } \\ { = \mathcal I ^ { T } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \mathcal { J } }$ is the Jacobian matrix $\boldsymbol { J } = [ \widehat { \sigma } \mathbf { Y } / \partial \mathbf { X } ]$ Also

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } ^ { * } ) = \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } + \mathbf { x } ^ { * } ) } \\ & { \qquad = \mathbf { Y } _ { o } - \{ \mathbf { Y } _ { c } ( \mathbf { X } ) + J \mathbf { x } ^ { * } + \cdot \cdot \mathbf { \cdot } \} } \\ & { \qquad \approx \mathbf { y } - J \mathbf { x } ^ { * } , } \end{array}
$$

where terms of order (x\*)² are neglected. Then the linearized form of (I.18) becomes

$$
\xi ^ { \mathcal { T } } Q ^ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \mathbf { y } - \mathcal { J } \mathbf { x } ^ { * } ) = \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { d } }
$$

or

$$
J ^ { \mathcal { T } } Q ^ { - \frac { 1 } { 4 } } J \mathbf { x } ^ { \ast } = J ^ { \mathcal { T } } Q ^ { - \frac { 1 } { 4 } } \mathbf { y } .
$$

These are the normal eguations

The best estimate $\mathcal { Z } ^ { \ast }$ is found fromthe solutio of (F.22), ie.,

$$
{ \bf x } ^ { * } = ( \bar { J } ^ { \bar { J } } Q ^ { - 1 } \bar { J } ) ^ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \bar { J } ^ { \bar { T } } Q ^ { - 1 } y = B { \bf y } ,
$$

where $\mathcal { B }$ is a linear estimator. $\mathbf { x }$ is a sample value of $\textbf { a }$ random variable $\pmb { \xi }$ T judge the usefulness of an estimate, we need its statistics,and in particular we need the covaniance matrix of $\pmb { \xi }$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad ^ { \circ } = E \left\{ \xi \xi ^ { T } \right\} } \\ & { \quad = E \left[ ( B \zeta ) ( B \zeta ) ^ { T } \right] } \\ & { \quad = B E \left[ \zeta \zeta ^ { T } \right] B ^ { T } } \\ & { \quad = B Q B ^ { T } } \\ & { \quad = B Q B ^ { T } } \\ & { \quad = \left( J ^ { T } Q ^ { - 1 } J \right) ^ { - 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

# Appendix J

# Notes on Hamiltonian Mechanics

For further details see Refs. 22, 26,and 27 for excellent introductory material and Ref.19 for applications.

# J.1 Elements of Lagrangian Mechanics

In this introductory summary we consider mechanical systems in which the forces can be derived from a potential. A system can be specified by a set of “generalized coordinates.” For instance,a rigid body requires six coordinates, three for the location of its center of mass and three for its orientation. These may be written as conventional coordinates, or specifed by the set q1; q2 · ,n of generalized coordinates. The system is then said to have n degrees of freedom. These are assumed to be independent; we ignore the possibility of constraints, i.e.,relations between the coordinates.

We can then write down an expression for the kinetic energy,T,in terms of these coordinates and their derivatives.For a single particle of mass m,in three dimensions,

$$
x = q _ { 1 } , y = q _ { 2 } , z = q _ { 3 } ; T = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } m ( { \dot { x } } ^ { 2 } + { \dot { y } } ^ { 2 } + { \dot { z } } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Or, using the spherical polar coordinates of section (5.6),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r = q _ { \bar { 1 } } , \hat { \theta } = q _ { \bar { 2 } } , \hat { \phi } = q _ { \bar { 3 } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
T = { \frac { \vec { \mathbb { i } } } { 2 } } m ( { \dot { r } } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } { \dot { \theta } } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta { \dot { \phi } } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

We assume that the dynamical system can be analyzed in terms of a system of masses,using,at first, Cartesian coordinates. Let each of these coordinates be expressed in terms of the generalized coordinates q1, q2,. , qn, and possibly the time t.Then

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = \dot { x } = \frac { \partial x } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { 1 } } \dot { q } _ { \bar { z } } + \cdots + \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { n } } \dot { q } _ { n } ,
$$

so that

$$
{ \frac { \partial { \dot { x } } } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } = { \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { k } } } .
$$

Therefore,

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \frac { \partial \dot { x } ^ { 2 } } { \partial \dot { q } _ { k } } \right) = m \frac { d } { d t } \left( \dot { x } \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { k } } \right) = X \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { k } } + m \dot { x } \frac { \partial \dot { x } } { \partial q _ { k } } ,
$$

where $X$ is the ${ \mathfrak { X } } ^ { \bullet }$ component of force acting on m. Now

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum m \left( \dot { x } ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } + \dot { z } ^ { 2 } \right) .
$$

So,adding all equations like (J.1.2) for al particles and allcoordinates,

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { k } } \right) = \sum \left( X \frac { \partial x } { \partial q _ { k } } + Y \frac { \partial y } { \partial q _ { k } } + Z \frac { \partial z } { \partial q _ { k } } \right) + \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { k } } .
$$

Suppose that the forces arise from a potential $\pmb { \ V }$ Then

$$
\sum ( X d x + Y d y + Z d z ) = - d Y .
$$

Then the right hand side of (J.1.3) can be writen as

$$
{ \frac { \partial ( T - V ) } { \partial g _ { k } } } .
$$

Now define the“Lagrangian”, or “Lagrangian function”

$$
\tilde { \mathcal { Z } } = \mathcal { T } - \mathcal { V }
$$

We then have the set of Lagrangian equations

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \left( { \frac { \partial { \bar { L } } } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } \right) - { \frac { \partial { \bar { L } } } { \partial q _ { k } } } = 0 , k = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

If $L$ does not contain the time explicitly, then we have the integral

$$
\sum { \dot { q } } _ { k } { \frac { \partial { \dot { \underline { { L } } } } } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } - { \bar { \underline { { L } } } } = { \bar { h } } .
$$

This can also be written as T+ V = h; so h is the total energy.

# J.2 Hamilton's Equations

Define the “monenta"

$$
p _ { k } = { \frac { \partial { \bar { L } } } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } .
$$

From Lagrange's equations,

$$
{ \dot { p } } _ { \bf k } = { \frac { \partial { \mathcal { L } } } { \partial g _ { \bf k } } } .
$$

Provided that the Hessian $| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } I } { \partial _ { q _ { i } } \partial _ { q _ { j } } } | \neq 0 ,$ ≠0,the equations (J.2.1) can be solved for the gk in terms of the coordinatesand monenta. Now define the“Hamiltonian”

$$
\tilde { H } ( q _ { 1 } , \ \cdots , \ q _ { n } , \ p _ { 1 } , \ \cdots \ p _ { n } , \ t ) = \sum \dot { q } _ { k } \frac { \partial \tilde { L } } { \partial \dot { q } _ { k } } - \tilde { L } ,
$$

where we assume that the substitution for the $\dot { \mathcal { G } } _ { \kappa }$ has been made.

Consider $\pmb { \mathcal { L } }$ geometrically in terms of the coordinates and momenta,we can write down the two differential forms:

$$
d L = \sum { \frac { \partial { \bar { L } } } { \partial q _ { k } } } d q _ { k } \div \sum { \frac { \partial { \bar { L } } } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } d { \dot { q } } _ { k } ,
$$

and

$$
d \left( \sum { \dot { q } } _ { k } { \frac { \partial L } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } \right) = \sum { \dot { q } } _ { k } { \dot { d } } p _ { k } \div \sum { \frac { \partial L } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { k } } } { \dot { d } } { \dot { q } } _ { k } .
$$

Then,using (J.2.2),we find

$$
d H = \sum ( { \dot { q } } _ { k } { \dot { d } } { p } _ { k } - { \dot { p } } _ { k } { \dot { d } } q _ { k } ) .
$$

From this,it follows that

$$
{ \dot { q } } _ { k } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { k } } } , { \dot { p } } _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { k } } } , k = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

These are Hamilton's equations of motion,often called “canonical equations."

If $\pmb { \mathcal { H } }$ does not contain the time explicitly, then

$$
H = h
$$

a constant: $H$ is the total energy.

$\boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } }$ is the column vector $x = [ q _ { 1 } \ q _ { 2 } \cdots q _ { n } \ p _ { 1 } \ p _ { 2 } \cdots p _ { n } ] ^ { \mathbb { T } } ,$

$$
{ \bar { J } } = \left[ { \frac { \bar { O } _ { \pi } ~ { \bar { J } } _ { \pi } } { - \bar { I } _ { \pi } } } \right] ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \bar { H } _ { x } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \partial H / \partial x _ { 1 } } \\ { \partial H / \partial x _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { \partial H / \partial x _ { 2 n } } \end{array} \right] , } \end{array}
$$

then the canonical equations can be written as

$$
\bar { \mathfrak { x } } = \bar { \mathfrak { z } } \bar { \mathfrak { z } } .
$$

Suppose that we have a time-dependent Hamiltonian

$$
H ( q _ { 1 } , \cdots , q _ { n } , p _ { 1 } , \cdots , p _ { n } , t ) .
$$

Let $q _ { 1 2 + 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ and define

$$
K ( q _ { 1 } , \cdots , q _ { n } , q _ { n + 1 } , p _ { 1 } , \cdots , p _ { n } , p _ { n + 1 } ) = H + p _ { n + 1 } .
$$

The canonical equations for the Hamiltonian $\kappa$ contain those for the Hamiltonian $H$ This means that results valid for time-independent (conservative) Hamiltonians can be easily generalized to time-dependent Hamiltonians.

# J.3 Canonical Transformations

Suppose that a transformation from $\pmb { \mathscr { x } }$ to $\mathfrak { Y }$ is given by

$$
y _ { k } = y _ { k } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , x _ { 2 n } ) , k = 1 , 2 , \cdot \cdot \cdot , 2 n .
$$

Here we shall assume that all functions have continuous second derivatives in theregion of interest. For this transformation we have the Jacobian matrix:

$$
M = \frac { \partial y } { \partial x } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \frac { \partial y _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \textrm { --- } \frac { \partial y _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 2 \pi } } } \\ { \vdots \vdots \vdots \vdots \vdots \vdots \vdots } \\ { \frac { \partial y _ { 2 \pi } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \textrm { --- } \frac { \partial y _ { 2 \pi } } { \partial x _ { 2 \pi } } } \end{array} \right]
$$

If at some point the Jacobian $\| \mathcal { B } \| \neq 0$ ,then in the neighborhood of that point equations (J.3.1) can be inverted uniquely, giving

$$
x _ { k } = x _ { k } ( y _ { 1 } , \ y _ { 2 } , \cdots , y _ { 2 n } ) , k = 1 , 2 , \cdots , 2 n .
$$

The Jacobian matrix of this transformation is $\lambda ^ { f - 1 }$ ，

Consider a transformation from $\{ \underset { \ P } { \Vec { \mathfrak { q } } } , \ p \}$ to $( Q , P )$ ，with Jacobian non-zero at all points in a given region,so that the transformation is invertiblein that region.This transformation is said to be canonical if for euery Hamiltonian function $H \{ q , p , t \}$ there exists another function $K ( Q , P , \notin )$ such that the transformation carries the canonical equations

$$
{ \dot { q } } _ { k } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { k } } } , { \dot { p } } _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { k } } } , { \dot { \kappa } } = { \vec { \bf { i } } } , { \bf { \nabla } } 2 , \cdot \cdot \cdot , n
$$

into

$$
{ \dot { Q } } _ { k } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial P _ { k } } } , { \dot { P } } _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial K } { \partial Q _ { k } } } , { \dot { k } } = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

Wintner (Ref.44) pp.22-26,proves the following theorem:This transformation is canonical $\vdots \Sigma$ and only if

$$
M ^ { \mathcal { T } } \mathcal { J } _ { i ^ { \mathcal { Y } } } ^ { \mathcal { M } } = \mu \mathcal { J }
$$

forp/0.Mis the Jacobian matrix of the transformation and Jis defined in (J.2.6).μ is called the “multiplier” of the transformation;the possibility that $\mu \neq \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ isignored in most textbooks.

If (J.3.6) is satisfed with μ= 1, then M is said to be symplectic.We shall show that MTJM = J isa suffcient condition for the transformation to be canonical. Let

$$
{ \mathfrak { x } } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { q } \\ { \mathfrak { p } } \end{array} \right] } \ { \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } } \ { \mathfrak { y } } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { Q } \\ { { \mathfrak { P } } } \end{array} \right] }
$$

Then

$$
\dot { y } = M \dot { x } \mathrm { a n d } \frac { \partial H } { \partial x } = M ^ { T } \frac { \partial K } { \partial y } , \mathrm { w h e r e } K ( y ) = H x ( y ) ,
$$

or the new Hamiltonian is the same as the old. (We continue to work with time-independent Hamiltonians.）Then,since $\dot { \mathfrak { x } } = \mathfrak { J } \tilde { \mathfrak { z } }$ ，

$$
\dot { y } = M J H _ { x } = M J M ^ { T } K _ { y } = J K _ { y }
$$

since $\pmb { \xi }$ issymplectic. This is what we had to show.

A condition for a canonical transformation is sometimes given in terms of Lagrange brackets or Poisson brackets. First, we comment that if M is symplectic,then so is $\mathbb { M } ^ { - 1 } = \partial \boldsymbol { x } / \partial \boldsymbol { y }$ Then

$$
\mathcal { I } = \left( \frac { \partial x } { \partial y } \right) ^ { T } J \left( \frac { \partial x } { \partial y } \right) = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial q } { \partial Q } \frac { \partial q } { \partial P } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial p } { \partial Q } \frac { \partial p } { \partial P } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle O _ { n } I _ { n } } \\ { \displaystyle - I _ { n } O _ { n } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial q } { \partial Q } \frac { \partial q } { \partial P } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial p } { \partial Q } \frac { \partial p } { \partial P } } \end{array} \right] ^ { T }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { = } & { \left[ \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { j } } \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { k } } - \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { k } } \right) \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { j } } \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial P _ { k } } - \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial P _ { k } } \right) \right] } \\ & { = \left[ \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial P _ { j } } \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { k } } - \frac { \partial p } { \partial P _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial Q _ { k } } \right) \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial P _ { j } } \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial P _ { k } } - \frac { \partial p _ { m } } { \partial P _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { m } } { \partial P _ { k } } \right) \right] } \\ & { \qquad = \left[ \displaystyle \frac { [ Q _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] [ Q _ { j } , P _ { k } ] } { [ P _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] [ P _ { j } , P _ { k } ] } \right] . } \end{array}
$$

This is the Lagrange matrix, and its components are Lagrange brackets. Its inverse is the Poisson matrix. A sufficient condition for a canonical transformation is, therefore,

$$
[ Q _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] = [ P _ { j } , P _ { k } ] = O _ { n } , [ Q _ { j } , P _ { k } ] = \tilde { I } _ { n } , [ P _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] = - \tilde { I } _ { n } , j , \tilde { \kappa } , = \tilde { 1 } , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

$\mathbb { N e x t } ,$ consider the differential form

$$
\sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { k } d q _ { k } - P _ { k } d Q _ { k } \right) ,
$$

and express it in terms of Qk and Pk. It becomes

$$
\sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { k } ( Q , P ) \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial q _ { k } } { \partial Q _ { j } } d Q _ { j } + \frac { \partial q _ { k } } { \partial P _ { j } } d P _ { j } \right) \right) - P _ { k } d Q _ { k } \right)
$$

$$
= \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } P _ { k } { \frac { \partial q _ { k } } { \partial P _ { j } } } \right) d P _ { j } + \left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } P _ { k } { \frac { \partial q _ { k } } { \partial Q _ { j } } } - P _ { j } \right) d Q _ { j } \right) = K .
$$

It is a long piece of work,but it is possible to show that the conditions that K be an exact differential form are all met using the properties of the Lagrange brackets given in (J.3.7).Consequently the transformation is canonical if and onlyif there is $a$ function $\mathbb { W } \{ \mathbb { Q } , \mathbb { P } \}$ whose total drivative is $\kappa$

# J.4 Canonical Transformations Defined by Functions

Let us start with a function $S ( q , Q )$ where the arguments are the old and new coordinates in a mapping from $\{ q , p \}$ to $( Q , \ P )$ $s$ is assumed to have continuous second derivatives. Then

$$
d S = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( { \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { k } } } d g _ { k } + { \frac { \partial S } { \partial Q _ { k } } } d Q _ { k } \right) . .
$$

Let the mapping be defined by the relations

$$
p _ { k } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { k } } } , \mathtt { } { P } _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial S } { \partial Q _ { k } } } , \mathtt { \ } { k } = 1 , \ 2 , \ \cdots , \mathtt { n } .
$$

This procedure guarantees that the differential form

$$
\chi _ { 1 } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { k } d q _ { k } - P _ { k } d Q _ { k } \right)
$$

in exact,so that the transformation is canonical. (We also need a condition that the equations (J.4.2) can be solved for ${ \mathfrak { q } }$ and $\mathfrak { P }$ in terms of $Q$ and $\pmb { F }$ This is the case if

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } S } { \partial q _ { j } \partial Q _ { k } } \Biggr \} \neq 0
$$

in the region of interest.)

The choice of q and Q as arguments of the generating function, S, is not essential. If K is exact, then so also will be

$$
K _ { 2 } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( q _ { k } d p _ { k } + P _ { k } d Q _ { k } \right) = d \left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \pi } ( p _ { k } q _ { k } ) \right) - K _ { 1 } .
$$

This leads to the canonical transformation:

$$
S = S ( p , Q ) : ~ q _ { \bar { k } } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial p _ { k } } } , ~ P _ { k } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial Q _ { \bar { k } } } } , ~ \bar { k } = 1 , ~ 2 , ~ \cdots , ~ n .
$$

Similarly, we have two other exact forms K3 and K4.

$$
K _ { 3 } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } ( p _ { k } d q _ { k } + Q _ { k } d P _ { k } ) = d \left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } ( P _ { k } Q _ { k } ) \right) + K _ { 1 }
$$

leads to

$$
S = S ( g , p ) : p _ { k } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial p _ { k } } , Q _ { k } = - \frac { \partial S } { \partial P _ { k } } , k = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

and

$$
K _ { \pm } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } ( q _ { k } d p _ { k } - Q _ { k } d P _ { k } ) = d \left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } ( p _ { k } q _ { k } ) \right) - K _ { 3 }
$$

leads to

$$
S = S ( p , P ) : q _ { k } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial p _ { k } } } , Q _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial S } { \partial P _ { k } } } , k = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

# J.5 The Hamilton-Jacobi Equation

Consider the time-dependent Hamiltonian

$$
H { \big ( } q _ { 1 } , \cdots , q _ { n } , \ p _ { 1 } , \cdots , p _ { n } , \ { \dot { \ s } } { \big ) } .
$$

As before, let qn+1 =t,and introduce the new Hamiltonian

$$
K ( q _ { 1 } , \cdots , q _ { n } , \ q _ { n + 1 } , \ p _ { 1 } , \cdots , p _ { n } , p _ { n + 1 } ) = H + p _ { n + 1 } .
$$

Apply to this the transformation induced by the generating function

$$
S ( g _ { 1 } , \dots , g _ { n } , q _ { n + 1 } , Q _ { 1 } , \dots , Q _ { n } ) ,
$$

where Qn+1 does not appear. For the time-independent Hamiltonian K, we know that the new and old Hamiltonians will be equal. As part of the transformation.

$$
\mathcal { P } _ { n + \frac { 1 } { 4 } } = \frac { \partial \mathcal { S } } { \partial \mathfrak { g } _ { n \div 1 } } = \frac { \partial \mathcal { S } } { \partial \mathfrak { t } } ,
$$

so if we return to the original notation, we have the following summary:


<!-- chunk 0007: pages 241-250 -->
Hamiltonian: $H ( q , p , \ t )$

Generating function: $S ( q , Q , \ t )$ ,where $\begin{array} { r } { \mathfrak { p } = \frac { \partial \mathcal { S } } { \partial \mathfrak { g } } } \end{array}$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { \vec { \mathcal { P } } = - \frac { \partial S } { \partial Q } } \end{array}$

New Hamitonian: $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { H } ^ { * } = \mathbb { H } + \frac { \partial S } { \partial \xi } } \end{array}$

A motivation for carrying out a canonical transformation is that the new canonical equations will be easier to solve than the old. Carrying this to an extreme, we consider the possibility that H\*= 0. Then Qk and P will all be constants, so we set

$$
Q _ { k } = \alpha _ { k } , P _ { k } = \beta _ { k } , \mathtt { k } = \mathtt { i } , \mathtt { 2 } , \cdots , \mathtt { n } .
$$

Now

$$
p _ { \mathrm { k } } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { \mathrm { k } } } } , { \mathrm { ~ } } { \bar { \kappa } } = 1 , \ 2 , \ \cdots , \ n ,
$$

so $s$ must satisfy the partial differential equation

$$
\vec { y } \left( g , \frac { \partial S } { \partial g } , \frac { \ d H } { \ d t } \right) = 0 .
$$

This is the Hamilton-Jacobi equation. We are not, fortunately, looking for its general solution; we just want a solution

$$
S ( q _ { 1 } , \dots , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \alpha _ { n } , \dot { z } )
$$

which contains $\mathfrak { N }$ independent arbitrary constants, $\alpha _ { 1 } , \cdots , \alpha _ { n }$ Once found, $s$ determines the transformation

$$
P _ { k } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { k } } } , \beta _ { k } = - { \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } } , { \vec { k } } = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .
$$

These equations actually contain the solution of the problem.

# J.6 The Problem of Two Bodies

Using spherical polar coordinates, and equations (J.1.1), (J.2.1) and (J.2.3), we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { q _ { 1 } = r , \ q _ { 2 } = \theta , \ q _ { 3 } = \phi , \ p _ { 1 } = \dot { r } , \ p _ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } \dot { \theta } , \ p _ { 3 } = r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \dot { \phi } , } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad H = \cfrac { 1 } { 2 } \left( p _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cfrac { p _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \cfrac { p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right) - \cfrac { \mu } { r } . } \end{array}
$$

The Hamilton-Jacobi equation is

$$
\frac { \mathrm { i } } { 2 } \left( S _ { r } ^ { 2 } + \frac { S _ { \theta } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { S _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right) - \frac { \mu } { r } + S _ { t } = 0 ,
$$

where the subscripts denote partial differentiation. The equation is solved in the form

$$
S = S _ { 0 } ( t ) + S _ { 1 } ( r ) + S _ { 2 } ( \theta ) + S _ { 3 } ( \theta ) .
$$

Details can be found in Refs 19,26, 29.The solution can be written as

$$
\begin{array} { l } { S = - \alpha _ { 1 } t + \alpha _ { 3 } \phi + \displaystyle \int _ { r _ { o } } ^ { r } \left( 2 \alpha _ { 1 } + \frac { 2 \mu } { r } - \frac { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } d r } \\ { + \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } ( \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \theta ) ^ { 1 / 2 } d \theta . } \end{array}
$$

We are not concerned with the details of the solution, describing the actual motion,but with the interpretation of the canonical constants:

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \alpha _ { 1 } = - \mu / 2 \alpha , } } & { { \beta _ { 1 } = T , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \alpha _ { 2 } = \sqrt { a \mu ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , } } & { { \beta _ { 2 } = - \omega , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \alpha _ { 3 } = \sqrt { a \mu ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cos i , } } & { { \beta _ { 3 } = - \Omega . } } \end{array}
$$

A disadvantage of this set is that since the mean motion is a function of α, some derivatives result in the appearance of the time outside trigonometric terms.This is avoided in another set introduced by Delaunay.First, consider the mapping from

$$
( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \alpha _ { 3 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 3 } ) \ \mathrm { ~ t o ~ } \left( A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \mathring { A } _ { 3 } , \ B _ { 1 } , \ B _ { 2 } , B _ { 3 } \right)
$$

given by the generating function

$$
S = \alpha _ { \bar { 1 } } \dot { \varepsilon } - \mu ( - 2 \alpha _ { \bar { 1 } } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \hat { A } _ { \bar { 1 } } - \alpha _ { 2 } \hat { A } _ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } \hat { A } _ { 3 } .
$$

Then

$$
\mathcal { B } _ { \mathrm { l } } = - \frac { \partial S } { \partial A _ { \mathrm { l } } } = \sqrt { a \mu } , B _ { \mathrm { 2 } } = \alpha _ { 2 } , B _ { \mathrm { 3 } } = \alpha _ { 3 } .
$$

$$
T = \beta _ { \bar { 1 } } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { \bar { 1 } } } } = \dot { \tau } - \mu ( - 2 \alpha _ { \bar { 1 } } ) ^ { - 3 / 2 } A _ { \bar { 1 } } = \dot { \tau } - { \frac { \bar { 1 } } { n } } A _ { \bar { 1 } } ,
$$

so A1= n(t-T),the mean anomaly. Also -w= β= -A2,and -Ω= β= $- \mathbb { A } _ { 3 }$ The new Hamiltonian is

$$
\frac { \partial S } { \partial \dot { \varepsilon } } = \alpha _ { 1 } = - \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 { \tilde { L } } ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

where $\bar { \hat { \mathbf { z } } } = \mathcal { B } _ { \vec { \mathbf { z } } }$

Finally，, according to astronomical practice and tradition, we switch coordinates and momenta - the equivalent of changing the sign of the Hamiltonian.

We shall call this new Hamiltonian F. Then in Delaunay's notation, we have the following canonical set:

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { { \cal L } = \sqrt { a \mu } , } } & { { \ell = { \cal M } , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { { \cal G } = \sqrt { a \mu ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , } } & { { g = \omega , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { { \cal H } = \sqrt { a \mu ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cos i , h = \Omega , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
{ \cal F } _ { \vartheta } = \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 L ^ { 2 } } .
$$

# J.7Perturbed Motion

Suppose that the original equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \tilde { \mathbf { r } } \div \mu \frac { \tilde { \mathbf { r } } } { \gamma ^ { 3 } } = \nabla F _ { 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

Let

$$
{ \boldsymbol { F } } = { \boldsymbol { \xi } } _ { 0 } + { \boldsymbol { \xi } } _ { 1 } ^ { * } .
$$

The corresponding canonical equations will be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \frac { d { \cal L } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \ell } } & { \displaystyle \frac { d \ell } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial { \cal L } } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d G } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial g } } & { \displaystyle \frac { d g } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial G } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d H } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial h } } & { \displaystyle \frac { d h } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial { \cal H } } } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

Equations (11.9.9) can be derived from these.

For a.simple application consider Keplerian motion perturbed by a force varying inversely with the cube of r; then we can take

$$
\vec { \cal F } _ { 1 } = \frac { \varepsilon } { r ^ { 2 } } .
$$

The motion will be planar,so only the variables $\{ \bar { L } , \bar { G } , \bar { \bar { \varepsilon } } , g , \}$ need to be considered. Also. $E _ { I }$ is $\textbf { a }$ function of $I , G$ and $\pmb { \xi } .$ but not of $_ { g }$ We shall make a transformation from $\{ \bar { L } , \mathcal { G } , \bar { \ell } , \bar { g } \}$ -to $\left\{ \bar { L } ^ { \ast } , \bar { G } ^ { \ast } , \bar { \varepsilon } ^ { \ast } , g ^ { \ast } \right\}$ using a generating function $S ( \Sigma ^ { * } , \mathcal { G } ^ { * } , \bar { \Sigma } , g )$ This will be expanded in powers of $\varepsilon$ ：

$$
S = S _ { 0 } ( \bar { L } ^ { \ast } , G ^ { \ast } , i , g \rangle + \varepsilon S _ { 1 } ( \bar { L } ^ { \ast } , G ^ { \ast } , \bar { i } , - \rangle + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

The purpose $0 \frac { 8 } { 2 }$ the transformation is to make the new Hamiltonian, $\{ \vec { \pmb { \varepsilon } } ^ { * } \{ \pmb { \bar { \ L } } ^ { * } , \vec { \pmb { \varepsilon } } ^ { * } \}$ independent of $\tilde { \mathbf { f } } ^ { * }$ Thus $\sum ^ { * }$ and $G ^ { \ast }$ will be constants and $\big \{ \big \} ^ { \sharp }$ and $\mathfrak { G } ^ { * }$ will vary

secularly with the time. For ε= 0, the old and new coordinates would be the same,so

$$
S _ { 6 } = \mathbb { \tilde { L } ^ { * } } \mathbb { \tilde { \ell } } \div \mathbb { \tilde { G } ^ { * } } g ,
$$

Also if $\Sigma ^ { \ast }$ is expanded similarly in powers of $\varepsilon$ then

$$
F _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( \mathbb { Z } ^ { * } ) = F _ { 0 } \{ \mathbb { Z } ^ { * } \} .
$$

The full transformation is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \displaystyle \bar { L } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial \bar { L } } = L ^ { * } + \varepsilon \frac { \partial S _ { \bar { 1 } } } { \partial \bar { l } } + \cdots , } & { \displaystyle G = \frac { \partial S } { \partial g } = - G ^ { * } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \bar { l } ^ { * } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial \bar { L } ^ { * } } = \bar { l } + \varepsilon \frac { \partial S _ { \bar { 1 } } } { \partial \bar { L } ^ { * } } + \cdots , } & { \displaystyle g ^ { * } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial G ^ { * } } = g + \varepsilon \frac { \partial S _ { \bar { 1 } } } { \partial \bar { G } ^ { * } } + \cdots . } \end{array}
$$

Equating the new and old Hamiltonians,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( L ^ { * } ) + \varepsilon F _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( L ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) + \dots = } } \\ { { \displaystyle F _ { 0 } \left( L ^ { * } + \varepsilon \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial l } + \cdots \right) + \varepsilon F _ { 1 } \left( L ^ { * } + \cdots , G ^ { * } + \cdots , l , - \right) = \varepsilon F _ { 0 } } } \end{array}
$$

Expanding F and Fi in powers of ε, we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { F _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( L ^ { * } ) + \varepsilon F _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( L ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) + \cdots = F _ { 0 } ( L ^ { * } ) + \varepsilon \displaystyle { \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial L } } \div } } \\ { { \varepsilon F _ { 1 } \left( L ^ { * } + \cdots , G ^ { * } + \cdots , l , - \right) + \cdots . } } \end{array}
$$

Clearly,

$$
\vec { F } _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( \mathcal { L } ^ { * } ) = F _ { 0 } ( \mathcal { L } ^ { * } ) = \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mathcal { L } ^ { * 2 } } .
$$

Then,equating terms of order $\varepsilon$

$$
F _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( L ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial \tilde { L } ^ { * } } \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial l } + F _ { 1 } ( L ^ { * } , G ^ { * } , l , - ) .
$$

Now define the secular part of $\mathfrak { F } _ { 1 }$ by

$$
{ \cal F } _ { 1 s } = \frac { \mathrm { i } } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } { \cal F } _ { 1 } ( L , G , l , - ) d l = \frac { 1 } { a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

and the periodic part by

$$
\overline { { \xi } } _ { 1 p } = \mathcal { F } _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } - \mathcal { F } _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } s }
$$

Since the left hand side of (J.7.12) is independent of l, we set

$$
\left. \begin{array} { c } { { F _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( \tilde { L } ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) = F _ { 1 s } ( \tilde { L } ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) = \displaystyle { \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { \tilde { L } ^ { * 3 } G ^ { * } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial \tilde { L } ^ { * } } \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial \tilde { t } } + F _ { 1 p } ( \tilde { L } ^ { * } , G ^ { * } , \tilde { i } , - ) = 0 . } } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

The new Hamiltonian has now been found (to the order ε) and so has the determining function,where

$$
S _ { \vec { \imath } } = \int \bar { F } _ { \vec { \imath } \vec { p } } \bar { d } \vec { \imath } = \{ v - \bar { \imath } \} ,
$$

the equation of the center. With

$$
\vec { E } ^ { * } ( \vec { L } ^ { * } , G ^ { * } ) = \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 \vec { L } ^ { * 2 } } + \varepsilon \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { \vec { L } ^ { * 3 } G ^ { * } } ,
$$

Hamilton's equations have the solution L\*= const., G\* = const.,

$$
\bar { t } ^ { * } = i _ { 0 } + \left( \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { L ^ { * 3 } } + \varepsilon \frac { 3 \mu ^ { 2 } } { \bar { L } ^ { * 4 } G ^ { * } } \right) t , \mathrm { ~ } g ^ { * } = g _ { 0 } + \varepsilon \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { \bar { L } ^ { * 3 } G ^ { * 2 } } t ,
$$

giving the secular perturbations. Then, from

$$
S = { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { * } { \mathbb { \ell } } + G ^ { * } g + \varepsilon ( v - { i } )
$$

we have

$$
{ \cal L } = { \cal L } ^ { * } + \varepsilon \left( \frac { \partial v } { \partial l } - 1 \right) , G = G ^ { * } , l ^ { * } = l + \varepsilon \frac { \partial v } { \partial \bar { L } } , g ^ { * } = g + \frac { \partial v } { \partial G } ,
$$

to the first order in $\varepsilon$ $\frac { \partial v } { \partial \mathcal { Z } }$ can be found at once from the integral expression, (J.7.16), for S1.The remaining derivatives can be found by applying the chain rule using formulas for elliptic motion. This is done in Ref. 19,where the approach just used, is applied to the problem of the motion of an artificial satellite.This method was proposed by Poincareé,and applied to orbits of minor planets by von Zeipel; it is often referred to as the “von Zeipel method."

# Bibliography and References

# Chapter 1

There are many first-rate introductory texts in astronomy available, and a perusal of some of these will provide pleasure as well as information. For more detailed information on the astronomical background pertinent to the dynamics of the solar system and space flight,see:

1. Baker, R.M.L., and Makemson, M.W. An Introduction to Astrodynamics. New York: Academic Press, 1967.

For information on spherical astronomy,see:

2.Smart,W.M. Textbook on Spherical Astronomy.Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press,1956.   
3.Woolard, E.W.and Clemence,G.M. Spherical Astronomy. New York: Academic Press,1966.

For a gold mine of practical information, see:

4.Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Ephemeris and the American Ephemeris and Nautical Almanac. London: Her Majesty's Stationery Offce.First published in 1960, but updated in more recent printings.

Also refer to:

5. The Astronomical Almanac for the current year, issued by the Nautical Almanac Offices in the U.S.and Great Britain,and available through Washington: U.S.Government Printing Offce, or London: Her Majesty’s Stationery Office.

# Chapter 2

Many texts include introductions to vectors. But beware of those that define vectorial properties in terms of their components. The references quoted here are:

6.Pars, L.A. Introduction to Dynamics. Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press,1953.

7.Rutherford,D.E. Vector Methods.New York: Interscience Publishers,1954.   
8.Milne,E.A. Vectorial Mechanics. New York: Interscience Publishers, 1948.

# Chapter 3

See references such as 6,7, and 8.

# Chapter 4

Accounts of central orbit theory will be found in most texts on mechanics, such as6,or the following:

9.Ramsey,A.S.Dynamics. (Parts one and two.) Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press,1946.   
10.Whittaker,E.T.Analytical Dynamics. (4th edition.） Cambridge,England: CambridgeUniversity Press,1959.   
11.Moulton,F.R.An Introduction to Celestial Mechanics. New York:Macmillan,1914. Now available through Dover Publications,New York.

# Chapter 5

Much of the material in this chapter is based on:

12.Ramsey，A.S.Newtonian Attraction. Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press,1949.

See also 8. Discussions of the figures rotating and tidally distorted masses, with application to astronomy,are given in:

13.Jeans, J.H. Problems of Cosmogony and Stellar Dynamics. Cambridge, England:Cambridge University Press,1919.   
14.Jeans,J.H.Astronomy and Cosmogony. Cambridge, England:Cambridge University Press,1928.   
15.Chandrasekhar， S. Ellipsoidal Figures of Equilibrium. New Haven:Yale University Press,1969.

# Chapter 6

Discussions of the problem of two bodies are found in 11,and many other texts including:

16.Baker,R.M.L. Astrodynamics: Applications and Advanced Topics. New York:Academic Press,1967.   
17.Bate,R.B.,Mueller,D.D.andWhite,J.E.Fundamentals of Astrodynamics. NewYork:Dover Publications,1971.   
18.Battin,R.H. Astronautical Guidance. New York:McGraw Hill, 1964.

19.Brouwer,D.and Clemence,G.M.Methods of Celestial Mechanics. New York: Academic Press,1961. This is the authoritative text on classical celestial mechanics.

20.Escobal,P.R.Methods of Orbit Determination New York:Wiley,1965.

21.Escobal,P.R. Methods of Astrodynamics.New York: Wiley,1968.

22.Fitzpatrick,P.M.Principles of Celestial Mechanics. New York:Academic Press,1970.

23.Gauss, C.F. Theoria Motus. New York: Dover Publications. Originally published in 1809, this makes vivid reading even today,and communicates some of the flavor of Gauss’ genius.

24.Herget,P.The Computation of Orbits.Ann Arbor,MI:Edwards Bros.,Inc., 1948. The outstanding work of its time in this area by the outstanding practitioner in this field. It is by no means outdated.

25.Herrick, S.Astrodynamics,Iand II. New York:Van Nostrand,1971,1972

26.Plummer,H.C. An Introductory Treatise on Dynamical Astronomy. New York:Dover Publications,1960.First published in 1918,this is a classic,and still, for me,one of the best references available.

27.Pollard,H.Mathematical Introduction to Celestial Mechanics. Englewood Cliffs,N.J.:Prentice Hall,1966.Chapters I to III appear in CelestialMechanics,Mathematical Association of America,1976.This text is carefully presented to be mathematically rigorous;it is beautifully written,and very clear.In particular,it is accessible to those with no initial background in mechanics.

28.Roy, A.E. Orbital Motion. Bristol, England: Adam Hilger,Ltd.,1982.

29.Smart,W.M. Celestial Mechanics. Cambridge,England:Cambridge University Press,1953.

30.Taff,L.G.Celestial Mechanics.New York:Wiley,1985.   
31.Watson, J.C.Theoretical Astronomy. Philadelphia:Lippincott,1986.

Fora discussion of the solution of Kepler's equation，with different initial guesses, see:

32.Danby, J.M.A.and Burkardt,T.M.The Solution of Kepler's Equation,I and II.Celestial Mechanics,31, 95-107,317-328,1983；40,303-312, 1987.

K. Stumpff published his paper on universal variables in:

33.Stumpff,K. Neue Formeln and Hilfstafeln zur Ephemeridenrechung,Astr. Nach,275,108-128,1947.

S.Herrick worked independently，and published a paper that will be more accessible to most readers:

34.Herrick, S. Universal Variables. Astron. J. 70,309-315, 1965.

A fundamental paper is

35.Goodyear,W.H. Completely General Closed Form Solution for Coordinates and Partial Derivatives of the Two-Body Problem. Astron. J., 70, 189-192,1965.

Invention in the problem of two bodies is far from dead. For a recent original method see:

36.Shepperd,S.W. Universal Keplerian State Transition Matrix. Celestial Mechanics,35,129-144,1985.

Formulas for the Julian Date, and the solar coordinates are taken from:

37.Almanac for Computers, 1986. Nautical Almanac Offce, U.S. Naval Observatory. Washington: U.S. Government Printing Offce.

More extensive formulas for the Sun, Moon and Planets can be found in:

38.Van Flandern，T.C.and Pulkkinen,K.F. Low-Precision Formulas for Planetary Positions. Ap.J. Supp.,391-411,Nov.,1979.

# Chapter 7

Many of the texts mentioned for Chapter 6 contain material on this subject. In particular,see 24.Also:

39.Williams,K.P.The Caleulations of Orbits of Asteroids and Comets.Bloomington,IN:The Principia Press,1934.   
40.Tattersfield,D.Orbits for Amateurs with a Microcomputer. New York: Wiley，1984.

The section on Gauss’ method owes much to B.G. Marsden. His paper,which contains many additional references,and historical notes, is:

41.Marsden,B.G.Initial Orbit Determination:the Pragmatist's Point of View.Astr0n.,J.,90,1541-1547,1985.

Herget'smethod for finding a preliminary orbit with the use of more than three observations.is described in:

42.Herget,P. Computation of Preliminary Orbits. Astron. J.,70,1-3,1965.

Calculation of partial derivatives specifically for Keplerian motion is described in 19 and 24,with a very elegant set of formulas in 35.

Some discussion on the method of least squares will be found in 19 and 24.

# Chapter 8

See11,19,26 and 27.Also:

43.Szebehely，V.Theory of Orbits.New York:Academic Press,1967.   
44.Wintner,A. The Analytical Foundations of Celestial Mechanics. Princeton: Princeton University Press,1947.   
45.Lundberg,J., Szebehely,V.,Nerem,R.S.and Beal,B. Surfaces of Zero Velocity in the Restricted Problem of Three Bodies. Celestial Mechanics, 36,191-205,1985.

# Chapter 9

See 27 for an outstanding introduction to qualitative properties of motion in the problem of $\pmb { \mathcal { Z } }$ bodies.

A discussion of the practical applications of the equations of motion to the solar system is given by:

46.Porter, J.G. A Comparative Study of Perturbation Methods. Astron. J., 63,405-406,1958.

# Chapter 10

A great deal of basic information can be found in:

47.Interpolation and Allied Tables. Great Britain:Her Majesty's Nautical Almanac Office,1956.

For further information,and worked examples, see:

48.Planetary Coordinates for the Years 1960-1980. Great Britain: Her Majesty's Nautical Almanac Office,1958.

See also 19 and 24.

Texts on numerical analysis include:

49.Conte, S.D.and De Boor,C. Elementary Numerical Analysis. New York: McGraw Hill,1972.   
50.Henrici,P.Discrete Variable Methods in Ordinary Differential Equations. New York:Wiley,1963.

Fehlberg's RK 4(5) method was published in:

51.Fehlberg,E.Classical Fifth-,Sirth-，Seventh-，and Eighth-order Runge-Kutta formulas with stepsize control. NASA Technical Report 278, 1968.

This is described in detail, with program listings in FORTRAN, PASCAL and BASIC in:

52. Danby, J.M.A. Computing Applications to Differential Equations. Reston, VA:Reston Publishing Company, 1985.

# Chapter 11

See texts such as 16,17,26and 28,but especially,19 and 24.Many worked examples can be found in 48. For excellent sources of derivations, formulas and algorithms,see:

53.Moyer,T.D. Mathematical Formulation of the Double-Precision Orbit Determination Program (DPODP).NASA Technical Report 32-1527. Pasadena,CA:Jet Propulsion Laboratory,1971.

54.Spier,G.W.Design andImplementation ofModels for the Double Precision Trajectory Program (DPTRAJ). NASA Technical Memorandum 33- 451.Pasadena,CA:Jet Propulsion Laboratory,1971.

See also:

55.Brown, E.W. An Introductory Treatise on the Lunar Theory. New York: Dover Publications,1960.(First published in 1896).   
56.Brown,E.W.and Shook,C.A.Planetary Theory. Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press,1933.

Hansen's method for perturbations is described in 19. See also 48 and:

57.Musen,P.Modified Formulae for Hansen's Special Perturbations.Astron. J.,63,426-429,1958.

The planetary equations are used to solve, to the first order, the problem of the motion of an artificial satellite in the field of an oblate planet in:

58.Kozai,Y.The Motion of a Close Earth Satellite.Astron.J.,64,367-377, 1959.

Herrick's method for the variation of parameters is given in:

59.Herrick,S.A Modification of the “Variation of Constants”Method for Special Perturbations.P.A.S.P.60,321-323,1948.

He applied his method, and gives a general discussion of other methods in:

60.Herrick, S. Icarus and the Variation of Parameters.Astron.J., 58,156- 164,1953.

Herrick gives a discussion of the integral for M in:

61.Herrick, S. The Mean Longitude or Mean Anomaly in Perturbations by Variation of Constants. Astron.J., 56,186-188,1951.

Merton's method is given in:

62.Merton,G.A Modification of the Perturbation-of-Elements Method. M.N.R.A.S.,109,421-435,1949.

Poincare's method was first applied in:

63.Henon,M.and Heiles,C. The Application of the Third Integral:Some Numerical Experiments.Astron.J.,60,73-79,1964.

Some applications to astronomical topics can be found in:

64.Danby,J.M.A.High-Energy Orbitsin Barred Spirals.Astron.J.,72,919- 925,1967.   
65.Danby,J.M.A.Motion of a Satellite of a Very Oblate Planet. Astron. J., 73,1031-1038,1968.

# Chapter 12

See 55;also 19,26 and 29.

# Chapters 13 and 14

See 26, 29, and many texts on mechanics, including:

66.Routh,E.J.Advanced Dynamics of a System of Rigid Bodies. New York: Dover Publications,1955.

Also see:

67. Jeffreys, H. The Earth. Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press, 1952.   
68. Munk,W.H. and MacDonald, J.F. The Rotation of the Earth. Cambridge, England:Cambridge University Press,1960.   
69.Lambeck,K. The Earth's Variable Rotation. Cambridge, England:Cambridge University Press,1980.   
70.Kopal, Z., Ed. Physics and Astronomy of the Moon. New York: Academic Press, 1971.

# Appendix

71.Marsden, B.G. Catalogue of Cometary Orbits. Minor Planet Center, Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory,Cambridge,MA,1982.

72.Bretagnon,P.Theorie du mouvement de 'ensemble des planetes.Solution VSOP82. Astronomy and Astrophysics,114,278-288,1982.   
73.Meeus,J.Astronomical Formulae for Calculators.Richmond,VA:Willmann-Bell, 1979.   
74.Bretagnon,P.and Simon,J.Planetary Programs and Tables from-4000 to +2800. Richmond,VA:Willmann-Bell,1986.   
75.Carusi, A.,Kresak,L., Perozzi,E.and Valsecchi,G.B., Long-term Evolution of Short-period Comets.Bristol,England:Adam Hilger,1986.
